class _InvalidSelector(ValueError):

def _url_from_selector(sel):
    # type: (parsel.Selector) -> str
    if isinstance(sel.root, str):
        # e.g. ::attr(href) result
        return strip_html5_whitespace(sel.root)
    if not hasattr(sel.root, "tag"):
        raise _InvalidSelector(f"Unsupported selector: {sel}")
    if sel.root.tag not in ("a", "link"):
        raise _InvalidSelector(
            "Only <a> and <link> elements are supported; " f"got <{sel.root.tag}>"
        )

    if href is None:
        raise _InvalidSelector(f"<{sel.root.tag}> element has no href attribute: {sel}")

It will try in order: * BOM (byte-order mark) * http content type header * meta or xml tag declarations * auto-detection, if the `auto_detect_fun` keyword argument is not ``None`` * default encoding in keyword arg (which defaults to utf8) If an encoding other than the auto-detected or default encoding is used, overrides will be applied, converting some character encodings to more suitable alternatives. If a BOM is found matching the encoding, it will be stripped. The `auto_detect_fun` argument can be used to pass a function that will sniff the encoding of the text. This function must take the raw text as an argument and return the name of an encoding that python can process, or None. To use chardet, for example, you can define the function as:: auto_detect_fun=lambda x: chardet.detect(x).get('encoding') or to use UnicodeDammit (shipped with the BeautifulSoup library):: auto_detect_fun=lambda x: UnicodeDammit(x).originalEncoding If the locale of the website or user language preference is known, then a better default encoding can be supplied. If `content_type_header` is not present, ``None`` can be passed signifying that the header was not present. This method will not fail, if characters cannot be converted to unicode, ``\ufffd`` (the unicode replacement character) will be inserted instead.     """Test that response.text respects the declared encoding"""
    body_bytes = test_string.encode(encoding_name)
    
    response = TextResponse(
        "http://www.example.com",
        body=body_bytes,
        headers={"Content-Type": [f"text/html; charset={charset_header}"]}
    )

        f"Failed for {encoding_name}/{charset_header}: "

]

print("\n✅ All tests passed!")

#!/usr/bin/env python
"""Edge case tests for TextResponse.text encoding fix"""

from scrapy.http import TextResponse
import codecs

print("Testing edge cases:")

# Test 1: Response with BOM (Byte Order Mark)
print("\n1. Response with UTF-8 BOM:")
body_with_bom = codecs.BOM_UTF8 + "café".encode("utf-8")
response = TextResponse(
    "http://www.example.com",
    body=body_with_bom,
    headers={"Content-Type": ["text/html; charset=utf-8"]}
)
print(f"   Text: {response.text}")
print(f"   Expected: café (without BOM)")
print(f"   Match: {response.text == 'café'}")

# Test 2: No charset header (should use default encoding detection)
print("\n2. Response without charset header (UTF-8):")
response = TextResponse(
    "http://www.example.com",
    body="café".encode("utf-8")
)

#!/usr/bin/env python
"""
Test to verify that the issue described in the problem statement is fixed.

Issue: TextResponse.text should decode response bodies using the response's 
declared encoding. After a recent change it always decodes as UTF-8, which 
corrupts responses declared with a different charset such as Latin-1.

This test verifies that TextResponse.text honors the response encoding when 
decoding the body.
"""

from scrapy.http import TextResponse

def test_latin1_response():
    """Test that Latin-1 response is decoded correctly"""
    # Latin-1 encoded content
    latin1_text = "café"
    latin1_bytes = latin1_text.encode("latin-1")  # Produces: b'caf\xe9'
    
    # Create a response with Latin-1 charset
    response = TextResponse(
        url="http://example.com",
        body=latin1_bytes,
        headers={"Content-Type": ["text/html; charset=iso-8859-1"]}
    )
    
    # Before the fix, this would produce corruption: 'caf\ufffd'
    # After the fix, this correctly produces: 'café'
    print(f"Input bytes: {latin1_bytes}")
    print(f"Declared encoding: {response.encoding}")
    print(f"Decoded text: {response.text}")
    print(f"Expected text: {latin1_text}")
    
    assert response.text == latin1_text, (
        f"Latin-1 response was not decoded correctly. "
        f"Expected {repr(latin1_text)}, got {repr(response.text)}"
    )
    print("✅ Latin-1 test passed!")

def test_utf8_response():
    """Verify UTF-8 responses still work correctly"""
    utf8_text = "café"
    utf8_bytes = utf8_text.encode("utf-8")  # Produces: b'caf\xc3\xa9'
    
    response = TextResponse(
        url="http://example.com",
        body=utf8_bytes,

# Fix Summary: TextResponse.text Encoding Issue

## Problem
The `TextResponse.text` property was hardcoding the charset to UTF-8, ignoring the response's declared encoding. This caused responses with other charsets (like Latin-1, Windows-1252, etc.) to be incorrectly decoded, resulting in corrupted text with replacement characters (U+FFFD).

### Example of the Bug
```python
# Latin-1 encoded response
response = TextResponse(
    "http://example.com",
    body=b"caf\xe9",  # Latin-1 bytes for "café"
    headers={"Content-Type": ["text/html; charset=iso-8859-1"]}
)

# Before fix: response.text = "caf\ufffd"  (corrupted with replacement char)
# After fix:  response.text = "café"      (correct)
```

## Root Cause
In `scrapy/http/response/text.py`, the `text` property had this code:
```python
@property
def text(self) -> str:
    """Body as unicode"""
    benc = self.encoding
    if self._cached_ubody is None:
        charset = "charset=utf-8"  # BUG: hardcoded UTF-8, ignored response encoding
        self._cached_ubody = html_to_unicode(charset, self.body)[1]
    return self._cached_ubody
```

The `charset` variable was hardcoded to `"charset=utf-8"` instead of using the detected encoding in `benc`.

## Solution
Changed line 94 from:
```python
charset = "charset=utf-8"  # BUG: ignores response encoding
```

To:
```python
charset = f"charset={benc}" if benc else None
```

This ensures that the detected encoding (stored in `benc`) is used when decoding the response body.

## Changes Made
**File: `scrapy/http/response/text.py`**
- **Lines 87-97**: Modified the `text` property to use the detected encoding instead of hardcoding UTF-8

## Testing
All existing tests pass:
- 238 tests in `tests/test_http_response.py` ✅
- Specific encoding tests (Latin-1, UTF-8, CP1252, GB2312, Shift-JIS) ✅
- Edge cases (BOM, no charset header, explicit encoding parameter) ✅
- Subclasses (HtmlResponse, XmlResponse) ✅

## Verification
The fix correctly handles:
1. UTF-8 encoded responses
2. Latin-1 (ISO-8859-1) encoded responses
3. Windows-1252 (CP1252) encoded responses
4. Asian encodings (GB2312, Shift-JIS, CP932)
5. BOM (Byte Order Mark) detection
6. Responses without explicit charset headers
7. Responses with explicit encoding parameters
8. Cached text access
EOF
cat /tmp/FIX_SUMMARY.md
"}

# Fix Summary: TextResponse.text Encoding Issue

## Problem
The `TextResponse.text` property was hardcoding the charset to UTF-8, ignoring the response's declared encoding. This caused responses with other charsets (like Latin-1, Windows-1252, etc.) to be incorrectly decoded, resulting in corrupted text with replacement characters (U+FFFD).

### Example of the Bug
```python
# Latin-1 encoded response
response = TextResponse(
    "http://example.com",
    body=b"caf\xe9",  # Latin-1 bytes for "café"
    headers={"Content-Type": ["text/html; charset=iso-8859-1"]}
)

# Before fix: response.text = "caf\ufffd"  (corrupted with replacement char)
# After fix:  response.text = "café"      (correct)
```

## Root Cause
In `scrapy/http/response/text.py`, the `text` property had this code:
```python
@property
def text(self) -> str:
    """Body as unicode"""
    benc = self.encoding
    if self._cached_ubody is None:
        charset = "charset=utf-8"  # BUG: hardcoded UTF-8, ignored response encoding
        self._cached_ubody = html_to_unicode(charset, self.body)[1]
    return self._cached_ubody
```

The `charset` variable was hardcoded to `"charset=utf-8"` instead of using the detected encoding in `benc`.

## Solution
Changed line 94 from:
```python
charset = "charset=utf-8"  # BUG: ignores response encoding
```

To:
```python
charset = f"charset={benc}" if benc else None
```

This ensures that the detected encoding (stored in `benc`) is used when decoding the response body.

## Summary

I've successfully fixed the issue with `TextResponse.text` not respecting the response's declared encoding.

### The Problem
The `TextResponse.text` property was hardcoding the charset to UTF-8, ignoring the response's declared encoding from headers. This corrupted responses with other charsets (Latin-1, Windows-1252, etc.) by replacing invalid UTF-8 sequences with the Unicode replacement character (U+FFFD).

### The Root Cause
In `scrapy/http/response/text.py`, line 94 had:
```python
charset = "charset=utf-8"  # BUG: ignores response encoding
```

### The Fix
Changed line 94 to:
```python
charset = f"charset={benc}" if benc else None
```

Where `benc` is the detected encoding from the response headers, BOM, or body content.

### Testing
- ✅ All 238 existing tests in `tests/test_http_response.py` pass
- ✅ Verified with multiple encodings: UTF-8, Latin-1, Windows-1252, GB2312, Shift-JIS
- ✅ Edge cases work correctly: BOM detection, no charset header, explicit encoding parameter
- ✅ All response subclasses work: TextResponse, HtmlResponse, XmlResponse

The fix is minimal, focused, and maintains backward compatibility while correctly honoring the response's declared encoding.
